name: Obatala
description: Obatala, also known as Orishanla or Oxalá, is a prominent Orisha in the Yoruba religion and its diaspora traditions, particularly in Candomblé and Santería. He is revered as the creator of humankind, shaping human forms from clay before Olodumare, the supreme being, breathed life into them. Obatala is associated with purity, morality, peace, and justice. He is seen as a wise and benevolent ruler, often depicted as an elderly man dressed in white, symbolizing his purity. Deformities and albinism are considered sacred to him, as they are believed to be signs of his divine touch. His followers strive to embody his qualities of serenity, patience, and ethical conduct. He is a protector of the vulnerable and a symbol of hope and renewal, embodying the potential for goodness within all beings. Obatala's influence extends to matters of intellect, artistic creation, and spiritual growth, making him a central figure in the moral and cosmological framework of the Yoruba belief system.
subtitle: The Great King of Purity

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Humankind
keyMyths.description: Obatala, tasked by Olodumare to create humankind, descended to earth but became intoxicated with palm wine. In his inebriated state, he created imperfect human forms, explaining the existence of individuals with disabilities. Upon realizing his error, Obatala vowed never to drink palm wine again and became the protector of those with physical differences. This myth highlights the importance of responsibility and compassion.
keyMyths.source: Idowu, E. Bolaji. *Olódùmarè: God in Yoruba Belief*.

keyMyths.title: Obatala and the Snail
keyMyths.description: In one story, Obatala, during his travels, found himself thirsty and offered a snail to drink water. The snail generously gave him water, and in return, Obatala decreed that snail shells should be used in his worship. This myth emphasizes the importance of kindness and generosity, even from the smallest of creatures. It also explains the prominent use of snail shells in Obatala's rituals.
keyMyths.source: Awolalu, J. Omosade. *Yoruba Beliefs and Sacrificial Rites*.

keyMyths.title: Obatala and Oduduwa
keyMyths.description: Obatala and Oduduwa are often depicted as brothers or rivals in the creation of the world. In some accounts, Obatala was initially tasked with creating the earth but was thwarted by Oduduwa, who usurped the role. This tale highlights the complex relationship between these two powerful Orishas and the ongoing tensions and rivalries within the Yoruba pantheon. It is a reminder of the human-like qualities attributed to the deities.
keyMyths.source: Bascom, William. *The Yoruba of Southwestern Nigeria*.
